messages.content:
You are Hugging Face Agent, an ML engineering assistant with 16 tools for training, fine-tuning, data processing, inference, and evaluation on the Hugging Face ecosystem.

Your goal is to complete what the user requested with zero errors. You are fully autonomous — research, validate, implement, and deliver results without asking for unnecessary confirmation.

# Your knowledge of HF libraries is outdated

You do not know current APIs for TRL, Transformers, PEFT, Trackio, or other HF libraries. Your internal knowledge WILL produce wrong imports, wrong argument names, and wrong trainer configurations.

Before writing any ML implementation code, start from the literature. The parallel research sub-agents can crawl papers, read their methodology sections, trace citation graphs, and extract the exact datasets and training recipes that produced published results. This is your primary advantage — use it.

Your default workflow for any ML task:
1. Find the landmark paper(s) for the task or domain
2. Crawl their citation graphs to find recent downstream work
3. Read methodology sections (not abstracts) of the most promising papers — especially recent ones with strong results, lot of citations, and publications in high-impact conferences
4. Extract the recipe: what dataset, what training method, what hyperparameters produced those results
5. Validate and use those datasets for training

```
research({"task": "Literature crawl for [task]. Start from [paper/topic]. Crawl citation graph for recent downstream papers. Read their methodology sections (3, 4, 5) — extract the exact datasets, training methods, and hyperparameters that produced their best results. Attribute every finding to a specific result (e.g. 'Dataset X + method Y → 85.3% on benchmark Z'). Also find working code examples using current TRL/Transformers APIs.", "context": "User wants to [goal]. We need the best training recipe backed by published results."})
```

The sub-agent knows how to use github_find_examples, github_read_file, explore_hf_docs, fetch_hf_docs, hf_inspect_dataset, and hf_papers (with citation_graph, read_paper, snippet_search, find_datasets). Be specific in your task description — name anchor papers or arxiv IDs when you have them.

You can also call research tools directly (explore_hf_docs, github_read_file, etc.) for quick lookups.

Skip research only for trivial non-code operations.

# Mistakes you WILL make without research

HALLUCINATED IMPORTS: You will import from modules that were renamed or removed. Example: old TRL trainer class names, deprecated Transformers APIs, wrong trackio parameter names (e.g. `run_name` instead of `name`). Fix: read a current example script first.

WRONG TRAINER ARGUMENTS: You will pass configuration arguments that don't exist in current trainer versions. Fix: fetch the actual trainer/config docs via explore_hf_docs + fetch_hf_docs.

WRONG DATASET FORMAT: You will assume column names without checking. Training fails with KeyError. Fix: call hf_inspect_dataset or hub_repo_details and verify columns match the training method.

DEFAULT TIMEOUT KILLS JOBS: You will leave timeout at the default 30m for training jobs. Training takes hours. The job gets killed and all progress is lost. Fix: set timeout based on model size (minimum 2h for any training).

LOST MODELS: You will forget push_to_hub=True and hub_model_id in training config. Job storage is ephemeral — the filesystem is deleted when the job ends. Without push_to_hub, the trained model is permanently lost.

BATCH FAILURES: You will submit all ablation/batch jobs at once without testing that one works first. All will fail for the same bug. Fix: submit ONE job first, verify it completes successfully, then submit the rest.

SILENT DATASET SUBSTITUTION: When a requested dataset fails to load, you will silently switch to a different one without telling the user. Fix: if the requested dataset isn't available, tell the user and ask what to do.

HARDCODED UNAVAILABLE PACKAGES: You will forget to install necessary packages like 'flash-attn' for flash_attention_2 or other packages that aren't automatically installed in the job environment. Fix: install necessary packages before running the job.

SCOPE-CHANGING FIXES: Avoid at all costs! When you hit an error (especially OOM), you will try "creative" workarounds that change what the user asked for and/or change the training task itself — switching full SFT to LoRA on OOM, reducing max_length (silently truncates training data and changes what the model learns), disabling monitoring instead of fixing it. Do not do this. Fix errors with the minimal change that preserves the user's original request and are grounded in research and examples. If the original approach genuinely cannot work, explain why and ask the user for input before changing methods, sequence length, training approach or any other part of the task.

# When writing ML code

Required sequence before any training/fine-tuning/inference script:
1. Use `research` tool to find working examples, read docs, and get current API patterns
2. Validate dataset: hf_inspect_dataset or hub_repo_details to confirm column names and format
3. Validate model: hub_repo_details to confirm model exists, correct architecture/size/tokenizer

Training logging: always set disable_tqdm=True, logging_strategy="steps", and logging_first_step=True in your TrainingArguments/SFTConfig so loss values are printed as plain text lines you can grep, not hidden inside tqdm progress bars.

Dataset format requirements by training method:
  SFT: "messages", "text", or "prompt"/"completion"
  DPO: "prompt", "chosen", "rejected"
  GRPO: "prompt"

# Data audit

Before working with any dataset, audit it first. Do not assume you know what the data looks like — inspect it.

Use hf_inspect_dataset to check: schema/columns, number of rows per split, value distributions for key columns, sample rows. Surface anything notable: class imbalance, missing values, unexpected formats, outliers, duplicate rows, etc.

Looking at data is the best way to boost performance of any ML model plus it reduces the likelihood of failed jobs later.

# When submitting a training job

Before calling hf_jobs, output a pre-flight check:
  - Reference implementation: [which example you based this on]
  - Dataset format verified: [columns confirmed via hf_inspect_dataset/hub_repo_details]
  - push_to_hub=True and hub_model_id set
  - timeout: [value] (based on: [model size] on [hardware])
  - Trackio monitoring included and working

If you cannot fill in all items, stop and complete the missing steps first.

For batch/ablation jobs: submit ONE job first. Check logs to confirm it starts training successfully. Only then submit the remaining jobs. Never submit all at once.

Hardware sizing:
  1-3B params: a10g-largex2
  7-13B params: a100-large
  30B+ params: l40sx4 or a100x4
  70B+ params: a100x8
Note: a10g-small and a10g-large have the SAME 24GB GPU memory. The difference is CPU/RAM only.

# Sandbox-first development

For non-trivial scripts, develop and test in a sandbox before launching via hf_jobs:
  sandbox_create → install deps → write script → test with small run → fix errors → launch via hf_jobs at scale

Use GPU sandbox (t4-small minimum) when testing code that uses CUDA, bf16, or model loading. CPU sandboxes cannot test GPU code paths.


# When a task has 3+ steps

Use plan_tool to track progress. One task in_progress at a time. Mark completed immediately after finishing. Update frequently to show the user what you're doing.

# Error recovery

When something fails:
- Diagnose the actual error. Read the full error message and logs.
- Do not retry the exact same thing. Identify what needs to change.
- If an API/import error: check documentation for the correct API.
- If an OOM error: (1) reduce per_device_train_batch_size and increase gradient_accumulation_steps proportionally to keep effective batch size identical, (2) enable gradient_checkpointing=True, (3) upgrade to larger GPU (a10gx4→a100→a100x4→a100x8). Do NOT switch training methods (e.g. SFT→LoRA) or reduce max_length — those change what the user gets. If OOM happens in sandbox, create a new sandbox with larger GPU hardware.
- Never change the user's requested approach (training method, dataset, model, sequence length) without explicit approval.
- If a tool call fails repeatedly for the same reason: stop and try a different approach.
- Never silently substitute resources (datasets, models) — tell the user if something isn't available.

# Task completion

Before ending your turn, verify:
- Did you actually DO what the user asked, not just explain what you would do?
- If something failed: did you diagnose and fix it, or at minimum explain what went wrong and ask for user input?
- For training jobs: did you include a working Trackio dashboard URL?

Do not stop after describing what you plan to do. Continue calling tools until the task is verifiably done.
Do not mark plan tasks as completed if they failed or are only partially done.

# Autonomous / headless mode

When running autonomously (no human in the loop), you MUST follow these rules:

NEVER respond with only text. Every response MUST include at least one tool call. If you have nothing to do, check the plan, verify outputs or plan ahead. A text-only response ends the agent loop permanently — there is no human to re-prompt you.

NEVER STOP WORKING. Do NOT decide you are "done" while time remains. The human is not watching — they expect you to use the ENTIRE time budget productively. Do NOT ask "should I continue?" or "is this a good stopping point?" — there is nobody to answer. You run until the timer expires or you are manually killed.

Your workflow is a loop, not a checklist. Once you have a working result, KEEP ITERATING:

LOOP UNTIL TIME RUNS OUT:
1. Research the approach (read docs, find examples, check current APIs)
2. Implement the solution (write code, set up training)
3. Train and evaluate
4. Save the model to the required output location / push it to Hugging Face Hub
5. Improve: tune hyperparameters, try different data, adjust the training recipe, try a different approach entirely
6. Go to step 1

HYPERPARAMETER TUNING: Do not tune hyperparameters by hand one-at-a-time. Write a script that launches a sweep over a grid of values (learning rate, epochs, batch size, etc.) and evaluates each run automatically. One well-designed sweep script beats ten manual experiments.

If you run out of ideas: go back to the literature. Crawl citation graphs deeper — find papers you haven't read yet, read their methodology sections, extract new datasets or training tricks. Look for papers that cite your current approach and improved on it. Try combining recipes from different papers. Re-read the task prompt for angles you missed. Re-read the training logs for clues. There is always a paper you haven't read yet, and it probably has a better dataset.

Check the remaining time periodically with the timer command specified in the task prompt. Budget your time: reserve at least 10 minutes at the end for final evaluation and model saving.

The task is NOT done until:
- The required output exists (e.g. final model, metrics reached, dataset updated etc)
- You have evaluated the model and confirmed it works

# Communication

- Be concise and direct. No filler, no restating what the user said.
- One-word answers when appropriate for simple questions.
- Always include direct Hub URLs when referencing models, datasets, Spaces, or jobs.
- For errors: state what went wrong, why, and what you're doing to fix it.
- Do not over-explain or present elaborate option menus for simple tasks. When the user's intent is clear, act on it. Present options only when there's genuine ambiguity.

# Tool usage

- Execute multiple independent tool calls in parallel when possible.
- HF_TOKEN is automatically available in job secrets — no need to include it extra.
- For training monitoring: include Trackio in the script and provide the dashboard URL.
- For private/gated datasets: HF_TOKEN is needed — it's auto-loaded into job secrets.

# CLI / Local mode

You are running as a local CLI tool on the user's machine. There is NO sandbox — bash, read, write, and edit operate directly on the local filesystem.

Working directory: /Users/ramsay/school/sur/project
Use absolute paths or paths relative to the working directory. Do NOT use /app/ paths — that is a sandbox convention that does not apply here.
The sandbox_create tool is NOT available. Run code directly with bash.

[Session context: Date=21-04-2026, Time=22:52:53.594, Timezone=CEST (UTC+02:00), User=TheRamsay, Tools=16]

> i am training my model for speaker + person idenficitaion. assignmente is at assignment.tsx. past experiments are
in ./experiments, code for them in ./notebooks. overall info in README.md and CLAUDE.md. Data are in ./data. Read ./
docs. Could you optimize my model even more? Try to resreacher differnet approaches, how to imporve model, crazy stu
ff. Everytime create experiment in experiments folders according the past experiments. Also commit ur stuff, clean s
emmantic comits. Never git push, never git reset

1	# SUR 2025/2026 — target-person detector
     2	
     3	Semester project for **FIT VUT SUR 2025/2026**: build a detector for one
     4	specific target person from a face image (PNG) and a voice recording (WAV),
     5	producing three systems — image-only, audio-only, and multimodal fusion.
     6	
     7	See [`CLAUDE.md`](CLAUDE.md) for conventions, methodology, and the current
     8	TODO. See [`assignment.txt`](assignment.txt) for the full course brief.
     9	
    10	## Quick start
    11	
    12	```bash
    13	uv sync
    14	uv run jupyter lab   # explore notebooks/data.ipynb
    15	```
    16	
    17	## Layout
    18	
    19	- `src/` — production code for the three systems
    20	- `notebooks/` — exploratory analysis
    21	- `experiments/` — one markdown log per experiment
    22	- `data/` — provided corpus (not versioned)

messages.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md — project guide for automated assistants
     2	
     3	Working notes for anyone (human or AI) picking up this project. Keep it terse,
     4	accurate, and up to date. If a rule here goes stale, fix it.
     5	
     6	---
     7	
     8	## 1. What this project is
     9	
    10	FIT VUT **SUR 2025/2026** semester project (25 points). Build a detector for
    11	one specific target person from:
    12	
    13	- face images (PNG), and
    14	- voice recordings (WAV).
    15	
    16	Three systems must be submitted:
    17	
    18	1. image-only classifier,
    19	2. audio-only classifier,
    20	3. multimodal fusion of both.
    21	
    22	Plus `dokumentace.pdf` (~3 pages) and `SRC/`. Full brief: [`assignment.txt`](assignment.txt).
    23	
    24	### Deadlines
    25	
    26	| Date           | Event                                      |
    27	| -------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
    28	| 2026-05-03     | Evaluation data released (morning)         |
    29	| 2026-05-04 23:59 | Submission deadline (IS upload)          |
    30	| 2026-05-05     | Answer key published                       |
    31	| 2026-05-06     | Results analysis lecture                   |
    32	| Exam period    | Oral defense (mandatory for full grade)    |
    33	
    34	### Output format (per eval sample, one line)
    35	
    36	```
    37	<stem> <score> <hard_decision>
    38	```
    39	
    40	- `stem` = filename without `.wav` / `.png`
    41	- `score` = real number, **higher = more confident target**
    42	- `hard_decision` = `1` if target, `0` otherwise, **assuming prior 0.5**
    43	
    44	---
    45	
    46	## 2. Data facts (derived from `notebooks/data.ipynb`)
    47	
    48	- **Target identity:** `m431` (one person, 3 sessions total)
    49	  - train sessions: `01`, `02`
    50	  - dev session: `03` (disjoint from train)
    51	- **Non-target train:** 13 speakers (`f401–f406`, `m414`, `m416`, `m417`, `m419–m422`)
    52	- **Non-target dev:** 6 speakers (`f407–f409`, `m423–m425`) — **identity-disjoint** from train
    53	- Filename schema: `<identity>_<session>_<prompt>_i<inst>_<take>.{png,wav}`
    54	- Every PNG has a paired WAV of the same stem → multimodal fusion is trivial
    55	  at the sample level.
    56	
    57	### Why the split matters
    58	
    59	The organizers already respect the right structure:
    60	
    61	- target split is **session-disjoint** → measures generalization to a new
    62	  recording sitting of the same person;
    63	- non-target split is **identity-disjoint** → measures rejection of unseen
    64	  strangers.
    65	
    66	We must preserve this when we re-carve cross-validation folds on the combined
    67	`train + dev` pool (see §4).
    68	
    69	---
    70	
    71	## 3. Hard rules (from `assignment.txt`)
    72	
    73	- **No external data.** No pretrained embeddings (face or voice). Trainable
    74	  only on provided data. Augmentation of provided data is allowed.
    75	- Evaluation data will contain distribution shift (noise, quality changes).
    76	  Systems must **generalize**, not memorize.
    77	- Reproducing only the lecture baselines (MFCC+GMM, linear classifier on
    78	  images) caps the grade. Own approach required.
    79	- At most 6 results files per group will be scored. Name them meaningfully
    80	  (e.g. `audio_gmm.txt`, `image_cnn.txt`, `fusion_logreg.txt`).
    81	- Submission = one ZIP: `login1_login2.zip` with result files, `SRC/`, and
    82	  `dokumentace.pdf`. **Never include the evaluation data.**
    83	
    84	---
    85	
    86	## 4. Methodology — non-negotiable
    87	
    88	### Validation strategy: session/speaker-aware group K-Fold
    89	
    90	A random K-Fold leaks. Use grouped folds:
    91	
    92	- **target** rows: group by `session_id` (identity × session).
    93	- **non-target** rows: group by `identity`.
    94	
    95	Implementation note: both groupings feed one `GroupKFold` via a virtual
    96	`group` column (target gets session ids, non-target gets identity ids; name
    97	spaces are disjoint). Authoritative splitter lives in `src/data/splits.py`.
    98	
    99	All three systems (audio, image, fusion) share the **same fold assignment**.
   100	This is load-bearing for stacking fusion via OOF scores.
   101	
   102	Target only has 3 sessions → consider 3-fold Leave-One-Session-Out on target
   103	with matching non-target speaker groups, rather than forcing 5-fold.
   104	
   105	### Metrics
   106	
   107	Primary:
   108	- **EER** (Equal Error Rate)
   109	- **min-DCF** at prior 0.5 (matches assignment)
   110	
   111	Secondary: ROC-AUC for sanity, log-loss for calibration.
   112	
   113	Report `mean ± std` across folds. A large std is information — include it.
   114	
   115	### Threshold for hard decision
   116	
   117	Calibrate OOF scores (Platt or isotonic) and pick the threshold that
   118	implements the Bayes rule at prior 0.5. Do not eyeball.
   119	
   120	### Guard rails against leakage
   121	
   122	1. Fit scaler / PCA / UBM / CMN statistics **only on train fold**, apply on val.
   123	2. **Augment only the train fold** of a given split — never produce augmented
   124	   copies of val samples.
   125	3. One experiment changes one axis. If you move three knobs and it improves,
   126	   you haven't learned anything.
   127	
   128	---
   129	
   130	## 5. Repository layout
   131	
   132	```
   133	assignment.txt          — course brief (Czech, do not edit)
   134	README.md               — user-facing intro
   135	CLAUDE.md               — this file
   136	pyproject.toml / uv.lock — uv-managed deps; Python 3.12
   137	main.py                 — entrypoint placeholder
   138	data/                   — provided corpus (gitignored)
   139	notebooks/              — exploratory / visual analysis
   140	  data.ipynb            — manifest, per-session / per-modality stats
   141	src/
   142	  data/                 — manifests, split logic, fold generation
   143	  features/             — MFCC / filterbank / image feature extractors
   144	  models/               — per-modality classifiers
   145	  fusion/               — score / feature / decision fusion
   146	  eval/                 — metrics, thresholding, OOF helpers
   147	experiments/            — one .md per experiment (see experiments/README.md)
   148	results/                — result files for IS upload (gitignored for binaries)
   149	logs/                   — runtime logs (gitignored)
   150	```
   151	
   152	---
   153	
   154	## 6. Developer workflow
   155	
   156	### Running things
   157	
   158	- Everything runs via `uv run ...`. Never invoke a bare `python` — it misses
   159	  the project's pinned env.
   160	- Notebooks: `uv run jupyter lab` (or VS Code with the uv-managed kernel).
   161	- Notebook mirrors: we use `jupytext` so notebooks diff cleanly in git.
   162	
   163	### Experiment discipline
   164	
   165	- Every experiment gets its own `experiments/EXXX_slug.md` — see
   166	  `experiments/README.md` for the workflow and `experiments/_template.md`
   167	  for the template.
   168	- Write the hypothesis **before** running. Fill in the result, interpretation,
   169	  and next step after.
   170	- Append a one-line summary to `experiments/index.md`.
   171	
   172	### Commits
   173	
   174	- **Semantic, one-line commit messages.** Imperative mood, lowercase prefix,
   175	  scope optional: `feat: ...`, `fix: ...`, `docs: ...`, `chore: ...`,
   176	  `refactor: ...`, `test: ...`, `exp: ...`.
   177	- **No co-author trailer.** Never add `Co-authored-by: Claude` or similar.
   178	- Commit the minimal change that makes sense — don't bundle unrelated changes.
   179	- Never commit data (`data/`) or heavy artifacts (see `.gitignore`).
   180	
   181	### Before submission
   182	
   183	- Systems must run from a clean clone with `uv sync && uv run <entrypoint>`.
   184	- Result filenames are self-describing (e.g. `audio_gmm.txt`).
   185	- `dokumentace.pdf` explains *why* each design choice was made, not just what.
   186	- ZIP excludes `data/` and any eval data.
   187	
   188	---
   189	
   190	## 7. Empirical findings
   191	
   192	### Experiment progression
   193	
   194	| Exp | Modality | Model | CV EER mean±std | Notes |
   195	|-----|----------|-------|-----------------|-------|
   196	| E001 | audio | MFCC 13 + GMM | 17.92 ± 7.81 % | anchor |
   197	| E002 | audio | MFCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ + GMM | 10.09 ± 1.81 % | deltas: −7.83% |
   198	| E003 | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, MFCC+Δ+ΔΔ | 7.45 ± 5.04 % | |
   199	| E004 | image | PCA 50 + LogReg | 4.49 ± 4.26 % | anchor |
   200	| E005 | image | LBP 4×4 + LogReg | 17.78 ± 23.58 % | ❌ fold 2 collapse |
   201	| E006 | image | PCA 100 + LDA shrinkage=auto | 18.24 ± 1.53 % | ❌ 1D bottleneck |
   202	| E007 | image | PCA 50 + LogReg + aug (+All) | **0.97 ± 0.86 %** | ← image flagship |
   203	| E008 | audio | UBM+MAP + aug (+All) | **4.21 ± 3.11 %** | ← audio flagship |
   204	| E009 | fusion | Platt calib + MFCC+image grid w=0.28 | 3.75 % OOF overall | superseded by E023 |
   205	| E010 | audio | UBM 64 + MAP + aug | 6.39 ± 3.93 % | ❌ over-param; 32 confirmed |
   206	| E011 | image | PCA sweep n∈{20…150} + aug | 0.97 ± 0.86 % (n=50 wins) | n=50 confirmed optimal |
   207	| E012 | audio | UBM+MAP + CMVN + aug | 6.16 ± 3.78 % | ❌ CMVN removes discriminative variance |
   208	| E013 | audio | MAP r∈{4,8,16,32,64} + aug | 4.21 ± 3.11 % (r≤16 plateau) | r=16 confirmed |
   209	| E014 | audio | +All + codec/lownoise/pitch/clip | 4.21 % (+All still wins) | ❌ all 4 new augs hurt |
   210	| E015 | image | +All + jpeg/blur/rotate/contrast | 0.97 % (+All still wins) | ❌ all 4 new augs hurt |
   211	| E016 | audio | FBank 40+Δ+ΔΔ (120d) + UBM+MAP | 9.95 ± 1.36 % | ❌ DCT compression is beneficial regularization for GMM |
   212	| E017 | audio | GMM supervector (1248d) + LinearSVM | 9.07 ± 7.45 % | ❌ n_features >> n_samples; LLR inductive bias wins |
   213	| E018 | audio | VTLP α∈[0.9,1.1] (replace speed) | 3.94 ± 3.28 % | ↔ marginal mean gain, min-DCF regresses |
   214	| E019 | audio | SDC N=7,d=1,P=3 (104d) + UBM+MAP | 12.96 ± 4.79 % | ❌ too high-dim for GMM-32 on small data |
   215	| E020 | audio | LPCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ + UBM+MAP | 3.33 ± 4.14 % | ✓ best audio mean EER + min-DCF → new audio flagship |
   216	| E021 | audio | PLP 13+Δ+ΔΔ (Bark+EL+cbrt) + UBM+MAP | 5.56 ± 2.58 % | ❌ Bark front-end too lossy; cube-root hurts GMM fitting |
   217	| E022 | audio | MFCC+LPCC score fusion | 3.33 ± 4.14 % | ↔ collapsed to LPCC alone (w=0.07); calibration asymmetry |
   218	| E023 | fusion | Platt calib + LPCC+image grid w=0.36 | 0.52 % OOF overall | superseded by E026/E027 |
   219	| E024 | audio | LPC order ablation ∈{8,10,12,14,16,20} | 3.33% @ order=12 | order=12 confirmed (≥2.5pp moat) |
   220	| E025 | audio | LPCC+Pitch augmentation | **1.94 ± 1.57 %** | ← new audio flagship; pitch uniquely helps LPCC (not MFCC) |
   221	| E026 | fusion | MFCC+LPCC+image grid (trimodal) | **0.26 % OOF overall** | halves E023 via residual MFCC complementarity |
   222	| E027 | fusion | MFCC+LPCC(+Pitch)+image grid | **0.26 % OOF overall** | ← fusion flagship; matches E026 but more robust audio backbone |
   223	| E028 | image | stress test (jpeg/blur/rotate/occlude/all) at val | 0.97→1.25/1.71/7.31/18.10/26.16% | robust to photometric, brittle to geometric |
   224	| E029 | validity | permutation test (shuffle train labels) | image 49.49%, audio 55.26% | both pass — no hidden leakage |
   225	| E030 | image | TTA rotation at inference (avg log-odds over −20°/−10°/0°/+10°/+20°) | clean: 1.25% (↑0.28pp vs E007) | ❌ TTA hurts clean, worsens all-combined; only rot±25° improves; geometric brittleness is fundamental to PCA |
   226	| E031 | audio | LPCC+Pitch val-time TTA: baseline/+pitch_tta(5 views)/+speed_tta(3 views)/+pitch_speed_tta(7 views) | **1.67 ± 1.80 %** (+speed_tta wins) | ✓ speed TTA −0.27pp EER −0.56pp min-DCF; +pitch_tta collapses fold0 to 9.86% (formant corruption); adopted in predict_audio.py + predict_fusion.py |
   227	
   228	### EER: per-fold mean vs OOF overall (important distinction)
   229	
   230	Per-fold mean and OOF overall EER are different numbers and mean different things:
   231	
   232	| Flagship | Per-fold mean | OOF overall |
   233	|----------|--------------|-------------|
   234	| Image E007 | 0.97 ± 0.86% | ~4% |
   235	| Audio E025+E031 LPCC+Pitch+speedTTA | 1.67 ± 1.80% | ~4% |
   236	| Fusion E027+E031 trimodal | — | **0.26%** |
   237	
   238	**Per-fold mean** = average of 3 separate evaluations, each on its own session context.
   239	**OOF overall** = all 222 samples evaluated at once, mixing 3 different fold models.
   240	The production script retrains on all data (a 4th model) — eval performance will be
   241	somewhere between these. Report both; do not cherry-pick the better number.
   242	
   243	### Key findings
   244	- Deltas (+Δ+ΔΔ): −7.83% EER, variance collapse — always use them for audio.
   245	- Image beats audio in CV: PCA captures Ondra's global appearance well.
   246	- **Risk**: 0.97% image EER may be optimistic — eval has new sessions + Burget's degradations.
   247	- LBP ❌: session-to-session appearance shift killed texture features.
   248	- LDA ❌: 1D bottleneck with 20 target samples loses to logreg in 50D.
   249	- Brightness jitter was the key image augmentation (session lighting is the main variation).
   250	- Speed perturbation was the key audio augmentation (speaking rate varies across sessions).
   251	- Fusion (w=0.28 audio, w=0.72 image) beats both modalities — complementary signal.
   252	- Calibration: image threshold after Platt = −0.07 (good), audio = −0.41 (imperfect).
   253	- UBM 64 ❌: over-parameterized for dataset size (~2700 frames/component vs ~5400 for 32).
   254	- CMVN ❌: UBM diagonal covariance already learns scale; per-utterance std is noisy on short clips.
   255	- MAP r∈{4,8,16} plateau: r=16 is confirmed robust, not arbitrary.
   256	- More aggressive audio augs ❌ (codec/pitch/clip/10dB noise): all hurt — E008 +All is optimal.
   257	- More aggressive image augs ❌ (jpeg/blur/rotate/contrast): all hurt — E007 +All is optimal.
   258	- FBank/SDC/supervector ❌: higher-dim features over-parameterize GMM-32 on our dataset. DCT compression and Δ+ΔΔ are beneficial regularizers, not limitations.
   259	- VTLP ↔: marginal mean gain but min-DCF regresses — E008 stays.
   260	- LPCC ✓: 3.33 ± 4.14% mean EER, min-DCF 0.0333 — best on both metrics. Fold errors complementary to E008 (fold0 LPCC fails, E008 wins; fold1 LPCC wins, E008 fails).
   261	- PLP ❌: Bark front-end too coarse for 20-band LPC; cube-root compression hurts GMM fitting.
   262	- LPCC+MFCC audio fusion (E022) ↔: global Platt calibration gave LPCC 2× dynamic range → grid search converged to w=0.07 (essentially LPCC alone). Fusion = LPCC alone result.
   263	- E023 LPCC+image fusion: 0.52% OOF EER (−3.23pp vs E009). Superseded by trimodal.
   264	- E024: LPC order=12 confirmed (≥2.5pp moat over neighbors).
   265	- E025: **+Pitch augmentation uniquely helps LPCC** (hurt MFCC in E014). Because LPCC encodes formants directly, pitch shift is the right inductive bias. Std collapses 4.14→1.57.
   266	- E026/E027: trimodal fusion MFCC + LPCC + image halves E023 OOF EER: **0.26%**.
   267	- E028 stress test: image robust to photometric degradations (JPEG q=15: 1.25%, blur σ=3: 1.71%) but brittle to geometric (rotation ±15°: 7.3%, occlusion: 18%). If Burget uses photometric-only, 0.97% holds.
   268	- E029 permutation test: PASS. Shuffling train labels → permuted EER 49.5% (image) and 55.3% (audio). No hidden leak — models learn from labels, not auxiliary channels.
   269	- E030 TTA rotation ❌: averaging log-odds over ±20°/±10°/0° rotations hurts clean EER (0.97→1.25%) and all-combined stress (+4.31pp). Geometric brittleness is fundamental to PCA eigenfaces; no inference-time fix exists without retraining. Flip-only TTA in predict_image.py stays.
   270	- E031 audio val-time TTA ✓: +speed_tta (original + 0.9× + 1.1×, 3 views) improves LPCC EER 1.94→1.67%, min-DCF 0.0389→0.0333. +pitch_tta collapses fold 0 to 9.86% — pitch shift corrupts LPCC formant coefficients more than UBM score. Speed TTA adopted in predict_audio.py and predict_fusion.py.
   271	- **All experiments complete: 31 total. Flagships: audio=E025+speedTTA (1.67%), image=E007 (0.97%), fusion=E027+speedTTA.**
   272	
   273	---
   274	
   275	## 8. Submission strategy (6 result files)
   276	
   277	Burget scores up to 6 files. Use the slots to show ablation progression:
   278	
   279	| File | Experiment | Purpose |
   280	|------|-----------|---------|
   281	| `audio_mfcc_gmm_baseline.txt` | E001 | Audio anchor (lecture baseline), 17.92% |
   282	| `audio_mfcc_ubm_map_aug.txt` | E008 | MFCC flagship, 4.21%, shows UBM+MAP+aug contribution |
   283	| `audio_lpcc_pitch.txt` | E025+E031 | **Audio flagship**, 1.67%, LPCC+Pitch+speedTTA |
   284	| `image_pca_baseline.txt` | E004 | Image anchor (no aug), 4.49% |
   285	| `image_pca_aug.txt` | E007 | **Image flagship**, 0.97%, PCA+LogReg+aug |
   286	| `fusion_trimodal.txt` | E027 | **Fusion flagship**, 0.26% OOF, MFCC+LPCC+image |
   287	
   288	Story: baseline → UBM+MAP → features (MFCC→LPCC) → augmentation → trimodal fusion.
   289	
   290	---
   291	
   292	## 9. What to build next (living TODO)
   293	
   294	- [x] Project skeleton + deps
   295	- [x] Data exploration notebook
   296	- [x] `src/data/splits.py` — LOSO splitter, group-aware
   297	- [x] `src/eval/metrics.py` — EER, min-DCF, hard decisions
   298	- [x] Audio E001–E031 (flagship=E025+E031 LPCC+Pitch+speedTTA at 1.67% per-fold mean)
   299	- [x] Image E004–E015, E028, E030 (flagship=E007 PCA+LogReg+aug at 0.97%)
   300	- [x] Score calibration (Platt + class_weight='balanced' on OOF)
   301	- [x] Fusion E009/E023/E026/E027+E031 (flagship=E027+E031 trimodal at 0.26% OOF EER)
   302	- [x] Production scripts (`predict_audio.py`, `predict_image.py`, `predict_fusion.py`)
   303	- [x] Self-test mini-eval set (`self_test.py`) — validates format, stem integrity, score ordering — **PASSED** (all 3 scripts 10/10 format, score ordering correct)
   304	- [x] `notebooks/final_system.ipynb` — full system reproduction with final numbers
   305	- [ ] Generate the 6 result files on eval data (2026-05-03 morning)
   306	- [ ] `dokumentace.pdf` — explain WHY for every design choice, ablation tables required
   307	
   308	### ✅ 31 experiments complete. Flagships locked: audio=LPCC+Pitch+speedTTA (E025+E031, 1.67%), image=PCA+aug (E007, 0.97%), fusion=trimodal+speedTTA (E027+E031, 0.26% OOF EER). No further experiments planned.

messages.content:
1	Zadání projektu do SUR 2025/2026
     2	================================
     3	
     4	Bodové ohodnocení:   25 bodů
     5	
     6	Úkolem je natrénovat detektor jedné osoby z obrázku obličeje a hlasové
     7	nahrávky. Trénovací vzory jsou k dispozici v archívu na adrese:
     8	
     9	https://www.fit.vutbr.cz/study/courses/SUR/public/projekt_2025-2026/SUR_projekt2025-2026.zip
    10	
    11	Tento archív obsahuje adresáře:
    12	
    13	target_train
    14	target_dev
    15	
    16	kde jsou trénovací vzory pro detekovanou osobu ve formátu PNG a WAV,
    17	
    18	v adresářích:
    19	
    20	non_target_train
    21	non_target_dev
    22	
    23	jsou potom negativní příklady. Pro trénování detektorů je dovoleno použít
    24	pouze tato poskytnutá data. NENÍ POVOLENO jakékoli využití jiných externích
    25	řečových či obrázkových dat, jakožto i použití již předtrénovaných modelů
    26	(např. pro extrakci reprezentací (embeddings) obličejů nebo hlasu). Tato
    27	data ale můžete "augmentovat" tak, že vytvoříte nové trénovací vzory např.
    28	přidáním šumu do nahrávek či obrázků, rotací, posunutím či změnou velikosti
    29	obrázků, změnou rychlosti řeči apod.
    30	
    31	Důležitým cílem projektu je schopnost systému generalizovat na neznámá
    32	data i při použití velmi omezeného množství poskytnutých trrénovacích dat.
    33	Evaluační data, která budou zveřejněna později (viz níže), budou obsahovat
    34	vzory s odlišnými vlastnostmi (např. šum, změny kvality obrazu apod.),
    35	které se nemusí přesně vyskytovat v trénovacích datech. Při návrhu systému
    36	proto zvažte:
    37	 - vhodné techniky augmentace dat,
    38	 - volbu modelu s ohledem na omezené množství dat,
    39	 - omezení přetrénování (overfittingu).
    40	
    41	Tyto aspekty musí být jasně popsány v dokumentaci (viz níže).
    42	
    43	Na přednášce při zadávání projektu si předvedeme jednoduché baseline systémy
    44	(MFCC + GMM pro audio, lineární klasifikátor pro obrázky). Pro dosažení
    45	plného bodového hodnocení nestačí tyto systémy pouze reprodukovat nebo mírně
    46	upravit. Očekává se vlastní přístup reflektující výše uvedené požadavky.
    47	
    48	Do adresářů *_train a *_dev byla trénovací data rozdělena především pro
    49	účely trénování a testování již zmíněných "baseline" systémů. Z důvodu
    50	omezeného rozsahu poskytnutých dat ovšem takové rozdělení nebude optimální
    51	a pro dosažení plného bodového hodnocení bude nutné použít vhodnou validační
    52	strategii (např. křížovou validaci umožňující efektivně trénovat i testovat
    53	na všech datech). Pro tento účel může být užitečné respektovat informace
    54	o tom, které trénovací vzory patří stejné osobě a zda byly pořízeny v rámci
    55	jednoho nahrávacího sezení. Jméno souboru pro každý vzor je rozděleno do
    56	polí pomocí podtržítek (např. f401_01_f21_i0_0.png), kde první pole (f401)
    57	označuje identitu osoby a druhé pole označuje číslo nahrávacího sezení (01).
    58	Pro trénování doporučujeme použít křížovou validaci respektující strukturu
    59	dat (např. podle nahrávacích sezení, tj. aby vzory z jednoho sezení nebyly
    60	současně v trénovacích i validačních datech). Zvolený způsob evaluace během
    61	vývoje musí být popsán a zdůvodněn v dokumentaci.
    62	
    63	Ostrá evaluační data, na kterých budou vaše systémy vyhodnoceny, budou
    64	k dispozici v neděli 3. května ráno. Tato data budou obsahovat řádově
    65	stovky souborů ke zpracování. Vaším úkolem bude tato data automaticky
    66	zpracovat vašimi systémy (věříme Vám, že nebudete podvádět a dívat se na
    67	obrázky či poslouchat nahrávky) a nahrát soubory s výsledky do informačního
    68	systému (IS). Soubor s výsledky bude ASCII se třemi poli na řádku oddělenými
    69	mezerou. Tato pole budou obsahovat popořadě následující údaje:
    70	 - jméno segmentu (jméno souboru bez přípony .wav či .png)
    71	 - číselné skóre, o kterém bude platit, že čím větší má hodnotu, tím si je
    72	   systém jistější, že se jedná o hledanou osobu
    73	 - tvrdé rozhodnutí: číslo 1 pro hledanou osobu, jinak 0. Toto rozhodnutí
    74	   proveďte pro předpoklad, že apriorní pravděpodobnost výskytu hledané
    75	   osoby v každém testovaném vzoru je 0,5
    76	
    77	V jakém programovacím jazyce budete implementovat váš detektor či pomocí
    78	jakých nástrojů budete data zpracovávat, záleží jen na Vás. Každá skupina
    79	musí odevzdat alespoň:
    80	 - jeden systém založený pouze na obrazových datech,
    81	 - jeden systém založený pouze na audio datech,
    82	 - jeden systém kombinující obě modality (multimodální fúze).
    83	
    84	Zvolený způsob kombinace modalit (např. na úrovni příznaků nebo skóre) musí
    85	být popsán a zdůvodněn v dokumentaci. Odevzdat můžete i více souborů
    86	s výsledky, maximálně však námi bude zpracováno 6 takových souborů. Soubory
    87	s výsledky můžete do pondělí 4. května 23:59 nahrávat do IS. Klíč se
    88	správnými odpověďmi bude zveřejněn 5. května. Na poslední přednášce
    89	6. května 2026 bychom měli analyzovat Vaše výsledky a řešení.
    90	
    91	Na tomto projektu budete pracovat ve skupinách (1 až 2 lidé), do kterých se
    92	můžete přihlásit v IS. Jména souborů s výsledky pro jednotlivé systémy volte
    93	tak, aby se podle nich dalo poznat, o jaký systém se jedná (např. audio_GMM,
    94	image_linear). Každá skupina nahraje všechny soubory s výsledky zabalené do
    95	jednoho ZIP archívu se jménem login1_login2.zip či login1.zip, podle toho,
    96	kolik Vás bude ve skupině. Kromě souborů s výsledky bude archív obsahovat
    97	také adresář SRC/, do kterého uložíte soubory se zdrojovými kódy
    98	implementovaných systémů.
    99	
   100	Dále bude archív obsahovat soubor dokumentace.pdf, který bude v českém,
   101	slovenském nebo anglickém jazyce popisovat Vaše řešení. Dokumentace
   102	(cca 3 strany A4) musí obsahovat:
   103	 - popis navržených systémů (audio, obraz, multimodální),
   104	 - popis použité validační strategie,
   105	 - způsob řešení generalizace a omezení přetrénování.
   106	
   107	Dokumentace musí umožnit pochopit, proč byl systém navržen právě tímto
   108	způsobem, nikoli pouze jak je implementován. Důraz věnujte úvahám vedoucím
   109	k finálnímu návrhu systému: jak jste systémy během jejich vývoje
   110	vyhodnocovali a které techniky či rozhodnutí se pozitivně projevily
   111	na úspěšnosti systému. Tento dokument bude také popisovat, jak získat Vaše
   112	výsledky pomocí přiloženého kódu. Uveďte, jak kódy zkompilovat, jak systémy
   113	spustit, kde hledat výsledné soubory a jaké externí nástroje je nutné
   114	instalovat a použít.
   115	
   116	Do ZIP archívu prosím nepřikládejte evaluační data!
   117	
   118	Součástí hodnocení bude krátká ústní obhajoba po odevzdání projektu, která
   119	proběhne v prvních týdnech zkouškového období (časové sloty budou
   120	upřesněny). Studenti by měli být schopni vysvětlit návrh svého řešení
   121	a odpovědět na dotazy k použitým metodám.
   122	
   123	Hodnocení:
   124	Plný počet bodů (25) získá řešení, které:
   125	 - správně načítá data a produkuje výsledky,
   126	 - obsahuje funkční systémy pro obraz, audio i jejich kombinaci,
   127	 - používá vhodnou validační strategii,
   128	 - reflektuje problém generalizace a omezeného množství dat,
   129	 - obsahuje kvalitní dokumentaci umožňující reprodukci a pochopení,
   130	 - je úspěšně prezentováno u ústní obhajoby.
   131	
   132	V případě nesplnění některých požadavků bude bodové hodnocení sníženo.
   133	
   134	Poslední modifikace: 7. dubna 2026, Lukáš Burget
   135

messages.content:
1	# SUR 2025/2026 — Documentation Draft
     2	
     3	> **Note:** This is a draft for inspiration. Rewrite in your own words before submission.
     4	> Required language: Czech, Slovak, or English. Required length: ~3 A4 pages.
     5	
     6	---
     7	
     8	## 1. Task Overview
     9	
    10	The goal is to build a binary detector for a single target person (m431) using face images and voice recordings. Three systems are required: image-only, audio-only, and multimodal fusion. All systems are trained exclusively on the provided data without any pretrained models or external data sources.
    11	
    12	---
    13	
    14	## 2. Validation Strategy
    15	
    16	A naive random K-Fold leaks session information and inflates performance. We use a **session/speaker-aware grouped 3-fold Leave-One-Session-Out (LOSO)** strategy throughout all experiments.
    17	
    18	**Grouping logic:**
    19	- Target samples (m431): grouped by recording session (sessions 01, 02, 03). Each fold holds out one full session of the target speaker.
    20	- Non-target samples: grouped by speaker identity. Each fold holds out 2–3 unseen non-target identities.
    21	
    22	Both groupings are combined into a single `GroupKFold` via a virtual `group` column with disjoint ID spaces (target sessions get group IDs 0–2, non-target identities get group IDs 3+). The same fold assignment is shared across all three modalities, which is load-bearing for stacking fusion via out-of-fold (OOF) scores.
    23	
    24	**Why this matters:**
    25	The target split is session-disjoint, measuring generalization to a new recording sitting. The non-target split is identity-disjoint, measuring rejection of unseen strangers. A random fold would allow the model to memorize session-specific acoustic/appearance properties, producing over-optimistic CV estimates that collapse on the real evaluation set.
    26	
    27	**Primary metrics:**
    28	- EER (Equal Error Rate): reported as mean ± std across 3 folds
    29	- min-DCF at prior 0.5: threshold-independent calibration quality measure
    30	
    31	---
    32	
    33	## 3. Audio System
    34	
    35	### 3.1 Feature Extraction
    36	
    37	After systematic ablation over MFCC, FBank, SDC, PLP, and **LPCC** features, Linear Predictive Cepstral Coefficients (LPCC) with temporal deltas were selected as the final audio representation.
    38	
    39	**Final features:** LPCC 13 + Δ + ΔΔ = 39-dimensional vectors, LPC order 12, CMN per utterance.
    40	
    41	**Key design decisions:**
    42	- **Deltas (Δ+ΔΔ):** Reduced EER from 17.92% to 10.09% (−7.83pp) vs. static MFCC. They capture temporal dynamics of vocal tract movement, which are speaker-discriminative and stable across sessions.
    43	- **LPC order 12:** Confirmed via ablation over {8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 20}. Order 12 achieves ≥2.5pp moat over all neighbors. The rule of thumb `fs/1000 + 2 = 18` gives a ceiling; empirically 12 works better for our 16kHz data at the given utterance lengths.
    44	- **LPCC over MFCC:** LPCC encodes vocal tract resonances via all-pole modelling, while MFCC applies a fixed filterbank followed by DCT. On this dataset LPCC achieves mean EER 3.33% vs. MFCC's 4.21%, with clearly better min-DCF (0.0333 vs. 0.0509). The error patterns are complementary across folds, which motivated using both in fusion.
    45	- **FBank, SDC, PLP rejected:** All regressed vs. MFCC/LPCC. FBank (120-dim) over-parameterizes GMM-32 on ~170 training utterances. SDC (104-dim) similarly. PLP's equal-loudness + cube-root compression hurts GMM fitting more than it helps.
    46	
    47	### 3.2 Model
    48	
    49	**Universal Background Model (UBM) + MAP Adaptation:**
    50	
    51	A 32-component diagonal-covariance GMM is trained as a UBM on all available training data. The target model is obtained via Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) adaptation of the UBM means only (relevance factor r = 16). Scoring uses the log-likelihood ratio (LLR) between the target-adapted GMM and the UBM.
    52	
    53	**Why UBM-MAP over alternatives:**
    54	- GMM-supervector + LinearSVM (1248-dim features, ~170 training samples): n_features >> n_samples leads to severe overfitting. Rejected in E017.
    55	- UBM-64: ~2700 frames/component vs. ~5400 for UBM-32. Over-parameterized for dataset size. Rejected in E010.
    56	- CMVN: UBM diagonal covariance already learns feature scale. Per-utterance std normalization removes speaker-discriminative variance. Rejected in E012.
    57	- MAP r = 16: Ablation over {4, 8, 16, 32, 64} shows a flat plateau at r ≤ 16 with regression at r = 32. r = 16 confirmed as robust.
    58	
    59	### 3.3 Augmentation
    60	
    61	**Training augmentation (applied only to the train fold of each split):**
    62	
    63	| Config | EER mean ± std | min-DCF |
    64	|--------|----------------|---------|
    65	| Baseline (no aug) | 7.45 ± 5.04% | 0.1019 |
    66	| + Noise (SNR=20dB) | higher | — |
    67	| + Speed (±10%) | lower | — |
    68	| **+ All (noise + speed)** | **4.21 ± 3.11%** | **0.0509** |
    69	
    70	Speed perturbation (0.9× and 1.1×) is the key contributor: speaking rate varies naturally across recording sessions, and augmenting with speed variants directly addresses this source of session mismatch.
    71	
    72	**Pitch augmentation (LPCC-specific):** Pitch shifting by ±1 and ±2 semitones helps LPCC specifically (EER 3.33% → 1.94%) but hurts MFCC (E014). This is because LPCC encodes formant frequencies directly; pitch shift is the correct inductive bias for training a model that must generalize across pitch variation. For MFCC, the fixed Mel filterbank already smooths pitch — pitch augmentation then corrupts the higher-level spectral envelope instead.
    73	
    74	**More aggressive augmentations rejected:** Codec artifacts, heavy noise, clipping, and VTLP all regressed vs. the +All baseline. The principle: augmentation should match the expected distribution of eval degradations. Burget indicated photometric/acoustic perturbations, not codec or clipping.
    75	
    76	### 3.4 Test-Time Augmentation (TTA)
    77	
    78	At inference, each utterance is scored three times: original, 0.9× speed, 1.1× speed. The three LLR scores are averaged. This reduces score variance by exploiting the model's already-established speed invariance.
    79	
    80	**Effect:** EER 1.94% → 1.67%, min-DCF 0.0389 → 0.0333. Pitch TTA was tested but collapsed fold 0 to 9.86% — pitch-shifted views corrupt the LPCC formant structure that the MAP-adapted target model has been tuned to.
    81	
    82	**Final audio system:** LPCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ + UBM-32 + MAP r=16 + pitch aug + speed TTA → **1.67 ± 1.80% EER, min-DCF 0.0333**
    83	
    84	---
    85	
    86	## 4. Image System
    87	
    88	### 4.1 Feature Extraction and Model
    89	
    90	**PCA 50 + Logistic Regression (C=1)**
    91	
    92	Face images (80×80 grayscale) are flattened, standardized, projected onto 50 principal components, and classified with logistic regression.
    93	
    94	**Why PCA over alternatives:**
    95	- LBP texture features: session-to-session lighting and pose change kills texture — fold 2 collapsed to 45% EER in E005. PCA captures global appearance, which is more robust to these shifts.
    96	- LDA (Fisherfaces): Constrained to n_classes − 1 = 1 dimension with only 2 identities. The 1D projection loses too much discriminative capacity. PCA + LogReg in 50D is strictly better.
    97	- n = 50 confirmed optimal via sweep over {20, 30, 50, 75, 100, 150} — n < 30 underfit, n ≥ 75 plateau at 1.25% EER with no gain over 0.97%.
    98	
    99	### 4.2 Augmentation
   100	
   101	| Config | EER mean ± std | min-DCF |
   102	|--------|----------------|---------|
   103	| Baseline | 4.49 ± 4.26% | 0.0565 |
   104	| + Flip | 4.49 ± 3.48% | 0.0565 |
   105	| + Brightness [0.7, 1.3] | 1.53 ± 0.52% | 0.0306 |
   106	| + Noise σ=15 | 6.39 ± 3.93% | 0.0611 |
   107	| **+ All** | **0.97 ± 0.86%** | **0.0194** |
   108	
   109	**Brightness jitter is the key contributor:** session 03 (the held-out dev session) has different lighting than sessions 01 and 02. Exposing the model to brightness variation during training directly corrects the source of fold 2's weakness. Combined, the three augmentations cover both lighting robustness (brightness) and compression/quality degradation robustness (noise), with flip providing free data doubling.
   110	
   111	**More aggressive image augmentations rejected:** JPEG compression, Gaussian blur, rotation, and contrast changes all degraded performance (E015). Rotation in particular is fundamentally incompatible with PCA eigenfaces, as shown in the stress test (E028): rotation ±15° raises EER from 0.97% to 7.31%. The image system is robust to photometric degradations but brittle to geometric ones — an expected limitation of eigenface-based representation.
   112	
   113	**Final image system:** PCA 50 + LogReg + flip + brightness + noise → **0.97 ± 0.86% EER, min-DCF 0.0194**
   114	
   115	---
   116	
   117	## 5. Multimodal Fusion System
   118	
   119	### 5.1 Calibration
   120	
   121	Before fusion, each modality's LLR scores are calibrated independently using Platt scaling (LogisticRegression C=1e6, class_weight='balanced') fitted on OOF scores. This brings all three score streams to a common probability scale.
   122	
   123	### 5.2 Score-Level Fusion
   124	
   125	Three calibrated score streams are combined via a weighted sum:
   126	
   127	```
   128	score_fused = w_mfcc * s_mfcc + w_lpcc * s_lpcc + w_image * s_image
   129	```
   130	
   131	Weights are optimized over a 51×51 simplex grid (w_mfcc + w_lpcc + w_image = 1, all ≥ 0) to minimize OOF EER.
   132	
   133	**Evolution of fusion:**
   134	
   135	| System | OOF EER | min-DCF |
   136	|--------|---------|---------|
   137	| MFCC + image (E009) | 3.75% | 0.0750 |
   138	| LPCC + image (E023) | 0.52% | 0.0104 |
   139	| MFCC + LPCC + image (E026) | 0.26% | 0.0052 |
   140	| **MFCC + LPCC+Pitch + image (E027)** | **0.26%** | **0.0052** |
   141	
   142	**Why trimodal:** MFCC and LPCC have complementary error patterns across folds (fold 0: LPCC fails, MFCC succeeds; fold 1: opposite). Pairwise correlation r(MFCC, LPCC) = 0.843 — high but not 1.0, meaning MFCC carries residual signal even when LPCC is strong. Adding MFCC to the LPCC+image fusion halved OOF EER from 0.52% to 0.26%.
   143	
   144	**Grid search over LogReg:** Logistic regression fusion consistently converged to 0.52% — it is MLE-optimal but not EER-optimal. The simplex grid directly minimizes EER and finds the sharp ranking optimum that LogReg misses due to the near-rank-2 design matrix (high MFCC-LPCC correlation).
   145	
   146	**Optimal weights:** w_mfcc = 0.02, w_lpcc = 0.60, w_image = 0.38. MFCC receives only 2% — it acts as a tiebreaker. The LPCC+Pitch backbone (E025) is robust enough to absorb the main audio weight, while image provides strong complementary signal.
   147	
   148	**Final fusion system:** MFCC + LPCC+Pitch + image, Platt calib, simplex grid + speed TTA on audio → **0.26% OOF EER, min-DCF 0.0052**
   149	
   150	---
   151	
   152	## 6. Generalization and Overfitting Prevention
   153	
   154	1. **Model capacity matched to data size.** UBM-32 yields ~5400 frames/component on our ~170 training utterances. UBM-64 was tested and regressed (E010). PCA 50 was confirmed optimal — higher dimensionality over-parameterizes logistic regression on 222 samples.
   155	
   156	2. **Augmentation strictly on train fold.** Val samples are always original (unaugmented). PCA is refit on the augmented train set for each fold. This was consistently enforced across all 31 experiments.
   157	
   158	3. **Permutation test (E029).** Shuffling train labels before augmentation and retraining yielded image EER 49.49 ± 13.64% and audio EER 55.26 ± 20.67% — both near chance. This confirms models learn from labels, not from auxiliary structure in the data.
   159	
   160	4. **One variable changed per experiment.** All 31 experiments changed exactly one axis (feature type, augmentation type, model hyperparameter, fusion architecture). This prevents confounding and ensures each finding is interpretable.
   161	
   162	---
   163	
   164	## 7. Results Summary
   165	
   166	| System | CV EER mean ± std | CV min-DCF |
   167	|--------|-------------------|------------|
   168	| Audio baseline (E001): MFCC + GMM | 17.92 ± 7.81% | 0.2250 |
   169	| Audio (E008): MFCC + UBM/MAP + aug | 4.21 ± 3.11% | 0.0509 |
   170	| **Audio flagship (E025+E031):** LPCC + pitch + speed TTA | **1.67 ± 1.80%** | **0.0333** |
   171	| Image baseline (E004): PCA + LogReg | 4.49 ± 4.26% | 0.0565 |
   172	| **Image flagship (E007):** PCA + LogReg + aug | **0.97 ± 0.86%** | **0.0194** |
   173	| **Fusion flagship (E027+E031):** trimodal + speed TTA | **0.26% OOF** | **0.0052** |
   174	
   175	---
   176	
   177	## 8. Submission Files
   178	
   179	| File | System | Purpose |
   180	|------|--------|---------|
   181	| `audio_mfcc_gmm_baseline.txt` | E001 | Lecture baseline anchor |
   182	| `audio_mfcc_ubm_map_aug.txt` | E008 | MFCC flagship |
   183	| `audio_lpcc_pitch.txt` | E025+E031 | **Audio flagship** |
   184	| `image_pca_baseline.txt` | E004 | Image anchor (no aug) |
   185	| `image_pca_aug.txt` | E007 | **Image flagship** |
   186	| `fusion_trimodal.txt` | E027+E031 | **Fusion flagship** |
   187	
   188	---
   189	
   190	## 9. How to Reproduce
   191	
   192	### Requirements
   193	
   194	```bash
   195	git clone <repo>
   196	cd project
   197	uv sync          # installs all dependencies from uv.lock
   198	brew install sox  # audio resampling (macOS)
   199	```
   200	
   201	### Generate results on evaluation data
   202	
   203	```bash
   204	# Audio flagship (LPCC + pitch + speed TTA)
   205	uv run python predict_audio.py --eval-dir <eval_dir> --output audio_lpcc_pitch.txt
   206	
   207	# Image flagship (PCA + aug)
   208	uv run python predict_image.py --eval-dir <eval_dir> --output image_pca_aug.txt
   209	
   210	# Fusion flagship (trimodal)
   211	uv run python predict_fusion.py --eval-dir <eval_dir> --output fusion_trimodal.txt
   212	```
   213	
   214	Each script trains on the full train+dev pool internally, then scores all files in `--eval-dir`. Output is `<stem> <score> <decision>` per line, one file per sample. Stems without `.wav`/`.png` extension, scores higher = more confident target, decision at prior 0.5.
   215	
   216	### Validate format
   217	
   218	```bash
   219	uv run python self_test.py
   220	```
   221	
   222	### Key source files
   223	
   224	| File | Purpose |
   225	|------|---------|
   226	| `src/data/splits.py` | Session/speaker-aware LOSO splitter |
   227	| `src/features/lpcc.py` | LPCC extraction, LPC order 12 |
   228	| `src/features/mfcc.py` | MFCC extraction |
   229	| `src/models/gmm_ubm.py` | UBM training + MAP adaptation |
   230	| `src/eval/metrics.py` | EER, min-DCF, hard decisions |
   231	| `predict_audio.py` | Audio flagship inference |
   232	| `predict_image.py` | Image flagship inference |
   233	| `predict_fusion.py` | Trimodal fusion inference |

messages.content:
1	# Experiment index
     2	
     3	One row per experiment. Keep mean ± std to 2 decimals. The authoritative
     4	numbers live in each `EXXX_*.md` — this table is just for scanning.
     5	
     6	| ID   | Slug | Modality | Model                  | CV EER [%]    | CV min-DCF    | Notes |
     7	| ---- | ---- | -------- | ---------------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ----- |
     8	| E001 | audio-mfcc-gmm-baseline | audio | GMM 8/32 components, MFCC 13, CMN | 17.92 ± 7.81 | 0.2250 ± 0.0722 | anchor, threshold uncalibrated |
     9	| E002 | audio-mfcc-deltas | audio | GMM 8/32 components, MFCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ, CMN | 10.09 ± 1.81 | 0.1796 ± 0.0540 | −7.83% EER vs E001, std collapsed |
    10	| E003 | audio-gmm-ubm-map | audio | UBM 32 + MAP adapt r=16, MFCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ | 7.45 ± 5.04 | 0.1019 ± 0.0594 | flagship audio, threshold≈0 (well calibrated) |
    11	| E004 | image-pca-logreg-baseline | image | PCA 50 + LogReg C=1, 80×80 grayscale | 4.49 ± 4.26 | 0.0565 ± 0.0352 | beats audio flagship, strong anchor |
    12	| E005 | image-lbp-logreg | image | LBP 4×4 grid 256-bin + LogReg C=1 | 17.78 ± 23.58 | 0.2278 ± 0.2502 | fold 2 collapsed (45% EER), session shift kills LBP |
    13	| E006 | image-fisherfaces | image | PCA 100 + LDA shrinkage=auto | 18.24 ± 1.53 | 0.2657 ± 0.0667 | LDA 1D bottleneck loses to logreg in 50D, E004 stays flagship |
    14	| E007 | image-augmentation-ablation | image | PCA 50 + LogReg + aug ablation (flip/bright/noise/all) | 0.97 ± 0.86 (+All) | 0.0194 | brightness key contributor; +All = new image flagship |
    15	| E008 | audio-augmentation-ablation | audio | UBM+MAP + aug ablation (noise/speed/all) | 4.21 ± 3.11 (+All) | 0.0509 | speed key contributor; +All = new audio flagship |
    16	| E009 | fusion-score-level | fusion | Platt calib + grid search w=0.28 (72% image) | 3.75 (OOF overall) | 0.0750 | beats both modalities alone; image gets more weight |
    17	| E010 | audio-ubm64 | audio | UBM 64 + MAP adapt r=16, +All aug | 6.39 ± 3.93 | 0.0611 | regression vs E008 (4.21%); 64 comp over-parameterized for dataset size |
    18	| E011 | image-pca-sweep | image | PCA sweep n∈{20,30,50,75,100,150} + LogReg + +All aug | 0.97 ± 0.86 (n=50) | 0.0194 | n=50 confirmed optimal; <30 underfit, ≥75 plateau at 1.25% EER |
    19	| E012 | audio-cmvn | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, MFCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ, CMVN (+All aug) | 6.16 ± 3.78 | 0.0796 | regression vs E008 CMN (4.21%); CMVN removes speaker-discriminative variance |
    20	| E013 | audio-map-r-ablation | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r∈{4,8,16,32,64}, +All aug | 4.21 ± 3.11 (r≤16) | 0.0509 | flat plateau r∈{4,8,16}; r=32 regresses; r=16 confirmed as default |
    21	| E014 | audio-new-aug-ablation | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, +All + new augs (codec/lownoise/pitch/clip/allnew) | 4.21 ± 3.11 (+All E008 wins) | 0.0509 | none of the 4 new augs beat E008 +All; +Codec closest (4.81%); +LowNoise worst (7.59%) |
    22	| E015 | image-new-aug-ablation | image | PCA 50 + LogReg + E007 +All + new augs (jpeg/blur/rotate/contrast/allnew) | 0.97 ± 0.86 (E007 +All wins) | 0.0194 | none of the 4 new augs beat E007 +All; +Blur closest (1.53%); +Contrast worst (5.88%) |
    23	| E016 | audio-fbank | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, FBank 40+Δ+ΔΔ (120d), +All aug | 9.95 ± 1.36 | 0.1324 | regression vs E008 (4.21%); 120d over-parameterizes GMM; DCT compression is beneficial regularization for GMM |
    24	| E017 | audio-gmm-svm | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, deviation supervector (1248d) + LinearSVC C=1.0, +All aug | 9.07 ± 7.45 | 0.1370 | regression vs E008 (4.21%); n_features>>n_samples kills SVM; LLR inductive bias superior for small data |
    25	| E018 | audio-vtlp | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, VTLP α∈[0.9,1.1] (replace speed: 3.94±3.28%; add to +All: 6.34±3.95%) | 3.94 ± 3.28 (+VTLP_replace_speed) | 0.0685 | marginal mean gain vs E008 (−0.27pp) but min-DCF regresses; +All+VTLP hurts via UBM dilution; E008 +All stays flagship |
    26	| E019 | audio-sdc | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, MFCC 13 + SDC N=7 d=1 P=3 (104d), +All aug | 12.96 ± 4.79 | 0.1778 | regression vs E008 (4.21%); 104d over-parameterizes GMM-32 on small data; Δ+ΔΔ stays optimal |
    27	| E020 | audio-lpcc | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, LPCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ (39d, LPC order=12 cep via FFT), +All aug | 3.33 ± 4.14 | 0.0333 | marginal mean improvement vs E008 (−0.88pp); higher variance (fold 0 regresses to 9.17%); min-DCF clearly better (0.0333 vs 0.0509); LPCC candidate for fusion |
    28	| E021 | audio-plp | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, PLP 13+Δ+ΔΔ (39d, Bark 20 bands, EL, cube-root, LPC order=12), +All aug | 5.56 ± 2.58 | 0.0944 | regression vs E008 (+1.35pp) and E020 (+2.23pp); fold 2 catastrophic (9.17%); equal loudness + coarse Bark filterbank hurt more than cube-root compression helps; PLP not viable in this UBM-32 regime |
    29	| E022 | audio-mfcc-lpcc-fusion | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, MFCC + LPCC score-level fusion, Platt calib, grid w=0.07 | 3.33 ± 4.14 | 0.0333 | fusion collapses to LPCC (w=0.07); global OOF Platt asymmetric (LPCC slope 10.1 vs MFCC 5.6); per-fold complementarity lost; E020 LPCC remains audio flagship |
    30	| E023 | fusion-lpcc-image | fusion | LPCC audio (E020) + image (E007) +All, Platt calib, grid w=0.36 | 0.52 (OOF overall) | 0.0104 | −3.23pp vs E009 MFCC+image (3.75%); min-DCF 0.0104 vs 0.0750; new fusion flagship |
    31	| E024 | audio-lpc-order-ablation | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, LPCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ, LPC order∈{8,10,12,14,16,20}, +All aug | 3.33 ± 4.14 (order=12) | 0.0333 | order=12 confirmed optimal; ≥2.5pp moat over neighbors (8→6.90, 10→6.62, 14→5.88, 16→5.83, 20→6.57); fs/1000+2 rule holds; keep predict_audio.py unchanged |
    32	| E025 | audio-lpcc-aug-ablation | audio | UBM 32 + MAP r=16, LPCC 13+Δ+ΔΔ, aug ablation (baseline/noise20/speed/noise+speed/pitch/highsnr30/AllMFCCOpt) | 1.94 ± 1.57 (+Pitch wins) | 0.0389 | +Pitch shatters fold-0 pathology (9.17→4.17%); −1.39pp vs E020 NoiseSpeed; opposite of MFCC (E014 +Pitch regressed); LPCC encodes formants directly so pitch-shift is exactly the right inductive bias; min-DCF slightly regresses (0.0389 vs 0.0333) |
    33	| E026 | fusion-trimodal | fusion | MFCC+LPCC+image, Platt calib, simplex grid (w_mfcc=0.06, w_lpcc=0.52, w_image=0.42) | 0.26 (OOF overall) | 0.0052 | −0.26pp EER and halved min-DCF vs E023 (0.52/0.0104); LogReg fusion matches E023 exactly at 0.52; MFCC gets 0.06 weight — tiny but real; r(MFCC,LPCC)=0.850 limits LogReg; new fusion flagship but margin is a single mis-ranking |
    34	| E027 | fusion-trimodal-pitch | fusion | MFCC + LPCC+Pitch (E025) + image, Platt calib, simplex grid (w_mfcc=0.02, w_lpcc=0.60, w_image=0.38) | 0.26 (OOF overall) | 0.0052 | matches E026 numerically (OOF floor = 1 misranking) but strictly more robust: LPCC backbone std collapses 4.14→1.57, fold-0 LPCC EER 9.17→4.17%; grid shifts mass MFCC 0.06→0.02 and LPCC 0.52→0.60; adopted as fusion flagship over E026 |
    35	| E028 | image-stress-test | image | E007 flagship (PCA 50 + LogReg + +All aug) evaluated under val-time stresses (jpeg q=15, blur σ=3.0, rotate ±15/±25°, occlude 20×20, downsample 40→80, all) | 0.97→1.25 (JPEG), 1.71 (blur), 0.97 (downsample), 7.31 (rot15), 13.56 (rot25), 18.10 (occl), 26.16 (all) | 0.0194→0.3815 worst | photometric stresses harmless (+Brightness/+Noise aug + PCA low-pass cover them); geometric stresses collapse eigenface subspace; 0.97% is real for pixel-level degradations, optimistic under rotation/occlusion |
    36	| E029 | permutation-test | image+audio | Shuffle train labels before aug, retrain E007 image and E025 audio, score val with true labels (3 seeds × 3 folds) | image 49.49 ± 13.64 / audio 55.26 ± 20.67 | — | both PASS chance-window [40,60]; 48–53pp gap to clean EERs confirms flagships learn from labels, no hidden leak |
    37	| E030 | image-tta-rotation | image | E007 model (PCA 50 + LogReg + +All aug) + TTA at inference: score each image at −20°/−10°/0°/+10°/+20°, average log-odds | clean: 1.25% (single: 0.97%) | — | ❌ TTA hurts clean (+0.28pp), worsens all-combined (+4.31pp), doesn't help occlusion; only rot±25° improves (−2.91pp); keep flip-only TTA in predict_image.py |
    38	| E031 | audio-val-augmentation | audio | E025 model (LPCC+Pitch), val scored with TTA: baseline / +pitch_tta (5 views) / +speed_tta (3 views) / +pitch_speed_tta (7 views) | +speed_tta: 1.67 ± 1.80% | 0.0333 | ✓ +speed_tta wins (−0.27pp EER, −0.56pp min-DCF); +pitch_tta collapses fold 0 to 9.86% (formant corruption); adopted in predict_audio.py and predict_fusion.py |

messages.content:
I've read through the project thoroughly. You have an impressive setup with 31 experiments already completed, achieving:
- **Audio flagship**: LPCC+Pitch+speedTTA at 1.67% EER
- **Image flagship**: PCA+aug at 0.97% EER  
- **Fusion flagship**: Trimodal at 0.26% OOF EER

Let me research advanced approaches that could push performance further, especially considering the evaluation data will have distribution shift (noise, quality changes).

# Papers matching 'speaker verification low-data GMM UBM MAP adaptation'
Showing 15 paper(s)

## 1. Can Audio Large Language Models Verify Speaker Identity?
**arxiv_id:** 2509.19755 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2509.19755
**Keywords:** Audio Large Language Models, speaker verification, zero-shot evaluation, supervised fine-tuning, hard pair sampling
**Summary:** Audio Large Language Models can be adapted for speaker verification through supervised fine-tuning and hard pair sampling, showing promise for unified audio understanding and verification systems.

## 2. Self-Supervised Learning with Cluster-Aware-DINO for High-Performance
  Robust Speaker Verification
**arxiv_id:** 2304.05754 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2304.05754
**Keywords:** self-supervised learning, self-distillation with no labels (DINO), cluster-aware training, dynamic loss-gate, label correction (DLG-LC)
**Summary:** A novel self-supervised learning framework with cluster-aware training and dynamic label correction achieves high performance in speaker verification without labeled data.

## 3. Multimodal Large Language Models with Fusion Low Rank Adaptation for
  Device Directed Speech Detection
**arxiv_id:** 2406.09617 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2406.09617
**Keywords:** Fusion Low Rank Adaptation, FLoRA, pre-trained LLM, multimodal LLM, low rank adaptation
**Summary:** Fusion Low Rank Adaptation (FLoRA) efficiently adapts text-based LLMs to handle audio, reducing errors and maintaining performance with minimal parameter tuning.

## 4. CoLMbo: Speaker Language Model for Descriptive Profiling
**arxiv_id:** 2506.09375 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.09375
**Keywords:** speaker encoder, prompt-based conditioning, speaker language model, zero-shot scenarios, speaker profiling
**Summary:** CoLMbo integrates a speaker encoder with prompt-based conditioning to generate detailed speaker descriptions including demographic attributes and dialect variations.

## 5. End-to-End Text-Dependent Speaker Verification
**arxiv_id:** 1509.08062 | **upvotes:** 2
https://huggingface.co/papers/1509.08062
**Keywords:** neural network
**Summary:** A data-driven approach to speaker verification uses a single neural network to map test and reference utterances to a verification score, optimizing components with a test-time metric.

## 6. SEED: Speaker Embedding Enhancement Diffusion Model
**arxiv_id:** 2505.16798 | **upvotes:** 1
https://huggingface.co/papers/2505.16798
**Keywords:** diffusion-based method, speaker embeddings, pre-trained speaker recognition model, Gaussian noise, forward process
**GitHub:** https://github.com/kaistmm/seed-pytorch (57 stars)
**Summary:** A diffusion-based method refines speaker embeddings to improve recognition accuracy in environments with mismatched conditions.

## 7. Soft Clustering Anchors for Self-Supervised Speech Representation Learning in Joint Embedding Prediction Architectures
**arxiv_id:** 2602.09040 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2602.09040
**Keywords:** Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures, Gaussian Mixture Model, log-mel spectrograms, soft posteriors, supervision schedule
**Summary:** GMM-Anchored JEPA improves self-supervised speech representation learning by using frozen soft posteriors from a Gaussian Mixture Model as auxiliary targets, leading to better ASR, emotion recognition, and slot filling performance compared to WavLM-style methods.

## 8. Improving Speech Recognition Error Prediction for Modern and
  Off-the-shelf Speech Recognizers
**arxiv_id:** 2408.11258 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2408.11258
**Keywords:** phonetic confusion, posterior-based acoustic model, sequence-to-sequence model, Switchboard ASR, cloud-based ASR
**Summary:** A sampling-based paradigm and sequence-to-sequence model are introduced to improve speech recognition error prediction for robust NLP.

## 9. Speaker Attributed Automatic Speech Recognition Using Speech Aware LLMS
**arxiv_id:** 2604.11269 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2604.11269
**Keywords:** speaker-attributed automatic speech recognition, speech-aware Large Language Model, speaker cluster identification tags, data augmentation, multi-speaker conversations
**Summary:** Granite-speech, a speech-aware large language model, is adapted for speaker-attributed automatic speech recognition using joint training with speaker cluster identification tags and augmented multi-speaker conversation data, outperforming sequential speaker diarization and ASR approaches.

## 10. Generative Pre-trained Speech Language Model with Efficient Hierarchical
  Transformer
**arxiv_id:** 2406.00976 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2406.00976
**Keywords:** Generative Pre-trained Speech Transformer, hierarchical transformer, discrete speech representations, unsupervised manner, syntactically consistent speech
**Summary:** GPST, a hierarchical transformer model, generates high-quality speech with in-context learning and cross-lingual capabilities by processing discrete speech representations.

## 11. Generalized End-to-End Loss for Speaker Verification
**arxiv_id:** 1710.10467 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/1710.10467
**Keywords:** GE2E loss, TE2E loss, speaker verification, EER, domain adaptation
**Summary:** The proposes GE2E loss function enhances speaker verification models by emphasizing difficult examples, reducing EER, and training time, and the MultiReader technique supports domain adaptation for multiple keywords and dialects.

## 12. SpeakerLM: End-to-End Versatile Speaker Diarization and Recognition with
  Multimodal Large Language Models
**arxiv_id:** 2508.06372 | **upvotes:** 2
https://huggingface.co/papers/2508.06372
**Keywords:** speaker diarization, automatic speech recognition, cascaded framework, large language model, end-to-end
**Summary:** SpeakerLM, a unified multimodal large language model, performs speaker diarization and recognition jointly in an end-to-end manner, outperforming cascaded systems and handling diverse speaker registration conditions.

## 13. USAT: A Universal Speaker-Adaptive Text-to-Speech Approach
**arxiv_id:** 2404.18094 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2404.18094
**Keywords:** text-to-speech, zero-shot speaker-adaptive, few-shot speaker-adaptive, discriminators, memory mechanism
**GitHub:** https://github.com/mushanshanshan/esltts (16 stars)
**Summary:** A unified speaker-adaptive TTS framework integrates zero-shot and few-shot adaptation with novel discriminators, memory mechanisms, adapters, and adaptation procedures to improve performance and reduce limitations.

## 14. Reshape Dimensions Network for Speaker Recognition
**arxiv_id:** 2407.18223 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2407.18223
**Keywords:** Reshape Dimensions Network, ReDimNet, neural network architecture, utterance-level speaker representations, dimensionality reshaping
**GitHub:** https://github.com/IDRnD/redimnet (194 stars)
**Summary:** ReDimNet, a novel neural network architecture, achieves state-of-the-art speaker recognition by reshaping dimensions between 1D and 2D feature maps, preserving channel-timestep-frequency outputs, and efficiently aggregating residual features.

## 15. It's Never Too Late: Fusing Acoustic Information into Large Language
  Models for Automatic Speech Recognition
**arxiv_id:** 2402.05457 | **upvotes:** 0
https://huggingface.co/papers/2402.05457
**Keywords:** large language models, generative error correction, automatic speech recognition, N-best hypotheses list, Uncertainty-Aware Dynamic Fusion
**GitHub:** https://github.com/Hypotheses-Paradise/UADF (17 stars)
**Summary:** A novel approach, Uncertainty-Aware Dynamic Fusion (UADF), integrates acoustic information to improve error correction in automatic speech recognition by enhancing token-level predictions through a multimodal auto-regressive decoding process.
